Since 2022, U.S. commercial banks have been steadily allowing their mortgage-backed securities (MBS) portfolios to run off. High interest rates have generated substantial unrealized losses, incentivizing banks to reduce duration risk and favor shorter-term assets like Treasuries or loans as they reinvest any available cash. Furthermore, the natural mechanism for MBS reduction—principal paydowns and prepayments—occurs automatically. In order for the total aggregate dollar amount to *increase* over a three-month horizon (October to January), banks would need to actively purchase new MBS in volumes large enough to exceed this natural run-off.

Even if Federal Reserve rate cuts materialize and lower yields, this would correspondingly accelerate MBS prepayment speeds as homeowners refinance. Faster prepayments mean the portfolios would shrink even quicker unless banks aggressively re-enter the MBS market. Given recent bank earnings guidance indicating a preference for maintaining flexibility and avoiding long-duration assets under current regulatory frameworks (like the Basel III end-game transition), a massive wave of net MBS purchasing is highly unlikely in the short term. The structural downward trend is strong enough to overshadow typical weekly noise. Consequently, the probability that the total MBS value will be higher on January 24th than it was on October 26th is relatively low.
